title: NIS2 Safety System Integrity Monitoring (SIL / SIS Interlocks)
exclusions: Does not determine legal applicability, complete regulator submissions, approve policy decisions, or replace counsel or competent-authority review. Does not replace onsite safety instrumented function engineering studies or proof-test execution—only visibility of telemetry Splunk receives.
evidence: Scheduled saved search `UC-22.2.40`, resilience dashboard panels, and archived CSV/PDF exports in `index=audit_evidence` showing bypass events joined to permit state.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing: SPL directly addresses Art.21(2)(c) requirements with specific, actionable monitoring.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(c) (Business continuity and crisis management) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.40: NIS2 Safety System Integrity Monitoring (SIL / SIS Interlocks).
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.40' running on index=sis_audit (HEC), index=itsm (permit-to-work), nis2_sis_critical_tags.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: A technician forces a valve interlock on `sis_id=SIS-REACTOR-A` while `snow:sc_task` shows no closed permit linked to that CMDB CI; the search emits at least one stats row with nonzero count and a null or non-Closed permit_state.
controlTest.negativeScenario: During a fully documented outage the same `sis_id` logs bypass strings but the related permit task is `Closed` within the search window, so the permit join excludes the event and the correlation yields no rows.

dataSources: `index=ot_safety` with `sourcetype=sis:status` (interlock state, trip bits, channel healthy flags; fields `sis_id`, `interlock_state`, `quality_flag`, `timestamp_source`). `sourcetype=edge_hub:modbus` from Splunk Edge Hub collectors carrying safety PLC registers (`register_address`, `value_raw`, `unit_id`). `sourcetype=sis:change_log` for configuration/bypass audit lines (`event_type`, `operator_id`, `peer_approved`). Splunk Enterprise Security ([Splunkbase 263](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/263)) for correlation; Splunk OT Security Add-on ([Splunkbase 5151](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/5151)) optional for asset context. Lookup `nis2_sis_inventory.csv`: `sis_id`, `sis_name`, `sil_level`, `last_test_date`, `test_cycle_months`.
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk OT Security Add-on (Splunkbase 5151)
spl:
(index=ot_safety (sourcetype=sis:status OR sourcetype=edge_hub:modbus OR sourcetype=sis:change_log)) earliest=-30d
| eval sis_id=coalesce(sis_id, controller_id, plc_name, unit_id)
| eval event_text=lower(coalesce(event_type, message, Operation, interlock_note, ""))
| lookup nis2_sis_inventory.csv sis_id OUTPUT sis_name, sil_level, last_test_date, test_cycle_months
| where isnotnull(sil_level)
| where match(event_text, "(?i)bypass|force|override|inhibit|maintenance.?mode") OR (sourcetype="sis:status" AND interlock_state IN ("defeated","bypassed","manual_override"))
| join type=left sis_id [
    search index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sc_task short_description="*permit*" earliest=-30d
    | stats latest(state) as permit_state latest(number) as permit_ticket by cmdb_ci
    | rename cmdb_ci as sis_id
]
| where permit_state!="Closed" OR isnull(permit_state)
| eval months_since_test=if(isnotnull(last_test_date), round((now()-last_test_date)/(30*86400),2), null())
| stats count values(event_text) as events max(months_since_test) as months_since_test by sis_id sis_name sil_level permit_state permit_ticket last_test_date test_cycle_months
| sort - count
description: Flags safety-system bypasses, forced interlock states, and configuration events that read as overrides when there is no closed maintenance permit aligned to the same engineering object; inventory fields surface proof-test age for triage but do not alone trigger the alert.
value: Helps the organisation prove that trip paths and safety overrides were either tied to authorised maintenance work or escalated quickly—reducing the chance that an unnoticed bypass becomes the root cause of a major physical incident.
implementation: Land historian and Edge Hub telemetry in `index=ot_safety`. Normalise `sis_id` across `sis:status`, `edge_hub:modbus`, and `sis:change_log`. Maintain `nis2_sis_inventory.csv` with proof-test cadence. Ingest ServiceNow permit tasks into `index=itsm` `sourcetype=snow:sc_task` (Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow, Splunkbase 1928 on HF). Schedule UC-22.2.40 daily with 30-day lookback; page OT/SIS SMEs on new unpermitted bypass clusters.
visualization: Table of sis_id, sis_name, sil_level, events, permit_state; timeline of bypass strings; single value count of open overrides; bar chart by plant if you add `plant_area` to the inventory lookup.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Alerts where nodename=Alerts.All_Alerts by Alerts.signature | head 20
dataModelAcceleration: Accelerate Alerts datamodel if ES notables will reuse signatures from this search; otherwise raw-index searches suffice.
knownFalsePositives:
• **Controlled shutdown with word “force” in vendor text** — Distinguish: benign alarm text contains force-load but `interlock_state` stays normal. Suppress: tighten `where` to require `interlock_state IN ("defeated","bypassed")` plus ticket join, or filter the SPL with `where NOT match(event_text,"force.?load")`.
• **PLC download during annual turnaround** — Distinguish: long maintenance window with hundreds of acknowledged changes. Suppress: `nis2_turnaround_windows.csv` time-bound exception lookup keyed on `plant_area` and `_time`.
• **Stale ServiceNow state** — Distinguish: permit closed in the field but `snow:sc_task` still shows Work in Progress. Suppress: reconcile via 15-minute accelerated summary of latest state; manual exception register row with ticket number until ITSM backlog clears.
• **Edge Hub duplicate register polls** — Distinguish: identical Modbus reads every **5 seconds** inflate counts. Suppress: `| bin _time span=1m | stats latest(value_raw) as value_raw by sis_id register_address _time` before bypass logic.
• **Proof-test schedule mismatch** — Distinguish: vendor moved digital twin tests off-cycle. Suppress: update `test_cycle_months` and `last_test_date` columns in `nis2_sis_inventory.csv` after signed SIS verification letter.
equipmentModels: IEC 61511 SIS controllers (Triconex, HIMA, Honeywell Safety Manager, Yokogawa ProSafe-RS)
Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928, change_request for SIS modification tracking)
Splunk Edge Hub (Modbus TCP/RTU and OPC-UA data collection from SIS controllers)
Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud (HEC for safety system telemetry ingest)
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• This UC implements NIS2 Art.21(2)(a) combined with operational safety obligations under IEC 61511 (functional safety for the process industry). Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS) are the last line of defence against hazardous events — they execute Safety Instrumented Functions (SIF) such as emergency shutdowns, pressure relief activation, and interlock trips. NIS2 requires entities in critical sectors (energy, water, chemical, manufacturing) to protect their network and information systems, which includes SIS controllers that are increasingly network-connected. A compromised SIS can either fail to trigger during a genuine hazard (safety failure) or trigger spuriously (operational disruption). Both outcomes are NIS2-relevant.
• **Critical safety concept**: Splunk monitors SIS telemetry but NEVER controls or modifies SIS logic. The UC detects anomalies in SIS state data — it does not interpret IEC 61511 safety logic. A functional safety SME must sign off on all field mappings and alert thresholds before production deployment. False positives are operationally preferable to missed detections in safety systems.
• **Data sources**:
  - `index=ot_safety sourcetype=sis:status` — periodic heartbeat from SIS controllers reporting interlock state, bypass status, diagnostic health, and firmware version. Collected via Splunk Edge Hub over Modbus TCP (function code 03 for holding registers, function code 02 for discrete inputs) or OPC-UA (node IDs for safety-certified data points). Key fields: `sis_id` (controller identifier, e.g., "SIS-UNIT-01"), `interlock_state` (armed, tripped, bypassed, maintenance, fault), `bypass_count` (number of active bypasses), `diagnostic_ok` (true/false — from the SIS self-test), `firmware_version`.
  - `index=ot_safety sourcetype=sis:change_log` — SIS engineering workstation audit trail. Emitted when an engineer modifies SIS logic, adds/removes interlocks, changes setpoints, or downloads firmware. Key fields: `sis_id`, `change_type` (logic_download, setpoint_change, bypass_add, bypass_remove, firmware_update), `engineer` (the username of the person who made the change), `change_ticket` (the associated MOC/change request number).
  - `index=ot_safety sourcetype=edge_hub:modbus` — raw Modbus register data from Edge Hub. Used for baselining and anomaly detection when `sis:status` heartbeats are not available from the SIS vendor's native export.
• **SIS controller vendors**: the major SIS platforms are Schneider Electric Triconex (TMR architecture), HIMA HIMax/HIMatrix (1oo2D architecture), Honeywell Safety Manager (TMR), and Yokogawa ProSafe-RS (1oo2 voting). Each has different register layouts, field naming conventions, and diagnostic indicators. The `sis:status` sourcetype normalises across vendors using `props.conf` field aliases. Before deployment, map the vendor-specific fields to the normalised schema and validate with the functional safety SME.
• **Safety integrity levels (SIL)**: SIS interlocks are rated SIL 1-4 (IEC 61511). SIL 3 and SIL 4 interlocks have the highest safety criticality — any anomaly on these interlocks requires immediate investigation. The `sis_sil_register.csv` lookup maps each `sis_id` to its SIL rating, associated SIF description, process unit, and owner.
• **Lookup**: `sis_sil_register.csv` with columns: `sis_id` (controller ID), `sil_rating` (1-4), `sif_description` (the Safety Instrumented Function, e.g., "High-pressure emergency shutdown", "Flame detection system"), `process_unit` (the plant area, e.g., "Unit 100 — Distillation"), `owner_email` (the process safety engineer responsible), `max_bypass_hours` (maximum allowed bypass duration before escalation — typically 8 hours for SIL 3, 2 hours for SIL 4).
• **Change management integration**: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928) pulls `change_request` records for SIS modifications. The UC correlates SIS change log events with approved change tickets — any SIS modification without a corresponding approved change ticket is a compliance finding (both NIS2 and IEC 61511).
• **RBAC**: role `nis2_safety_analyst` with `srchIndexesAllowed = ot_safety, itsm, audit_evidence`. SIS data reveals safety architecture details — extremely sensitive in critical infrastructure environments.
• **Index retention**: `index=ot_safety` ≥ 7 years (safety records may be required for incident investigations and regulatory proceedings years after an event).

Step 1 — Configure data collection
**Splunk Edge Hub for SIS heartbeats:**
Configure Edge Hub to poll SIS controllers via Modbus TCP. For a Triconex TMR controller:
- Protocol: Modbus TCP, port 502
- Function code: 03 (Read Holding Registers)
- Registers: 40001-40010 (interlock states — consult the Triconex I/O mapping document)
- Poll interval: 60 seconds (1-minute resolution is sufficient for compliance monitoring; do not poll faster than 10 seconds on safety-critical networks to avoid interference)
- HEC endpoint: `https://hec.splunk.example.com:8088/services/collector`
- Sourcetype: `edge_hub:modbus` (raw) or `sis:status` (if Edge Hub pipeline normalises the fields)

**CRITICAL**: before connecting Edge Hub to SIS network segments, obtain written approval from the functional safety manager. The SIS network is typically air-gapped or heavily segmented. Adding a data collection device requires a safety impact assessment per IEC 61511 clause 11.7.

Configure `props.conf` for SIS status normalisation (vendor-specific):
```ini
[sis:status]
KV_MODE = json
TIME_FORMAT = %Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S
FIELDALIAS-sis_id = controller_id AS sis_id
FIELDALIAS-interlock = safety_state AS interlock_state
EVAL-bypass_count = if(isnotnull(active_bypasses), active_bypasses, 0)
EVAL-diagnostic_ok = if(match(diagnostic_result, "(?i)pass|ok|normal"), "true", "false")
```

Verify:
```
index=ot_safety (sourcetype=sis:status OR sourcetype=edge_hub:modbus) earliest=-24h
| stats count dc(sis_id) as controllers values(interlock_state) as states by sourcetype
```
Expected: `controllers` matches the number of SIS controllers on the network. `states` should include "armed" (normal) and possibly "tripped" or "maintenance" for controllers under test.

**SIS change log:**
SIS engineering workstations (e.g., Triconex TriStation, HIMA SILworX) generate audit logs when engineers connect, download logic, or modify setpoints. Export these logs via syslog or file monitoring:
```ini
[monitor:///var/log/sis/change_audit.log]
sourcetype = sis:change_log
index = ot_safety
```

Verify: `index=ot_safety sourcetype=sis:change_log earliest=-90d | stats count dc(sis_id) as modified_controllers dc(engineer) as engineers values(change_type) as change_types`.

**SIL register lookup:**
Place `sis_sil_register.csv` in `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/TA-nis2-safety/lookups/`. Validate: `| inputlookup sis_sil_register.csv | stats count dc(sis_id) as controllers dc(sil_rating) as sil_levels values(sil_rating) as sil_list`.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Save as `UC-22.2.40 NIS2 Safety System Integrity`.

Understanding this SPL — design decisions:

• **Three detection conditions combined with OR**:
  - **Bypass anomaly**: `bypass_count > 0 AND interlock_state="bypassed"` — an interlock in bypass mode means the safety function is disabled. Bypasses are sometimes necessary during maintenance or testing, but must be time-limited and tracked. If `bypass_count` exceeds the registered `max_bypass_hours` (from the SIL register), the bypass has exceeded its allowed duration.
  - **Diagnostic failure**: `diagnostic_ok="false"` — SIS controllers perform periodic self-tests (proof tests). A failed diagnostic means the SIS may not function correctly during a demand. This is a high-priority finding for SIL 3/4 interlocks.
  - **Unauthorised change**: `change_type IS NOT NULL AND isnull(change_ticket)` — any SIS modification (logic download, setpoint change, bypass) without an associated approved change ticket is a compliance violation. NIS2 and IEC 61511 both require controlled change management for safety systems.

• **SIL-based severity escalation**: the lookup enrichment adds `sil_rating`. Alert routing escalates based on SIL: SIL 4 → immediate plant manager notification, SIL 3 → process safety engineer within 1 hour, SIL 2 → next shift review, SIL 1 → weekly review.

• **`coalesce(sis_id, controller_id, plc_name, unit_id)`**: normalises the controller identifier across different SIS vendors and sourcetypes. Triconex uses `controller_id`, HIMA uses `unit_id`, Honeywell uses `plc_name`.

Schedule: cron `*/15 * * * *` (every 15 minutes). SIS anomalies require rapid detection. Time range: use the embedded lookback in the SPL (30d for change log, 24h for status). Trigger: `Number of results > 0`.

Alert actions: (1) For SIL 3/4 interlocks with `diagnostic_ok=false`: PagerDuty page to the process safety engineer and plant shift supervisor. (2) For bypasses exceeding `max_bypass_hours`: email to the functional safety manager. (3) For unauthorised changes: email to the CISO and process safety manager — this is both a safety and cybersecurity incident. (4) Write all results to `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=nis2:sis:evidence`.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) **Bypass detection**: if possible in a lab/test SIS environment, activate a bypass on a test interlock. Verify the UC detects `interlock_state=bypassed` and `bypass_count > 0`. Remove the bypass. Verify the alert clears on the next run.

(b) **Diagnostic failure detection**: trigger a diagnostic failure on a test SIS controller (if supported by the vendor's test mode). Verify `diagnostic_ok=false` appears in the results.

(c) **Unauthorised change detection**: in a test environment, perform a setpoint change on a SIS controller without creating a ServiceNow change ticket. Verify the UC flags the change with `isnull(change_ticket)`.

(d) **SIL register coverage**: `| inputlookup sis_sil_register.csv | join type=left sis_id [search index=ot_safety sourcetype=sis:status earliest=-24h | stats count by sis_id] | where isnull(count)`. This identifies controllers in the register that have not reported status in 24 hours — a data collection gap.

(e) **Change ticket correlation**: for a known authorised SIS change (with an approved change ticket), verify the UC correctly matches the change to the ticket and does NOT flag it as unauthorised.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard (recommended name: `NIS2 Safety System Integrity`):
• **Row 1 — Safety posture**: Single-value `SIS controllers monitored` (count from register). Single-value `Active bypasses` (red ≥ 1 for SIL 3/4). Single-value `Diagnostic failures` (red ≥ 1). Single-value `Unauthorised changes (30d)` (red ≥ 1).
• **Row 2 — Interlock status table**: all SIS controllers with `sis_id`, `sil_rating`, `interlock_state`, `bypass_count`, `diagnostic_ok`, `last_heartbeat`. Colour-code: green=armed, amber=maintenance, red=bypassed/fault.
• **Row 3 — Bypass timeline**: `index=ot_safety sourcetype=sis:status interlock_state=bypassed earliest=-90d | timechart span=1d dc(sis_id) as bypassed_controllers`. Line chart showing bypass frequency over time.
• **Row 4 — Change audit trail**: `index=ot_safety sourcetype=sis:change_log earliest=-90d | table _time sis_id change_type engineer change_ticket`. Full change history for audit review.

Runbook (owner: Process Safety Manager / CISO):
1. Every 15 minutes (automated): investigate alerts. For SIL 3/4 bypasses:
   - Verify the bypass is authorised (linked to an approved change ticket and time-limited).
   - If unauthorised: immediately contact the plant shift supervisor. An unauthorised SIS bypass is both a safety violation (IEC 61511) and a cybersecurity concern (potential TRITON/TRISIS-style attack targeting safety systems).
   - If the bypass has exceeded `max_bypass_hours`: escalate to the functional safety manager for immediate removal or formal extension with risk assessment.
2. Weekly: review all diagnostic failures. For persistent failures, schedule a proof test per IEC 61511 clause 16.
3. Monthly: review the change audit trail. Verify all SIS changes have corresponding approved MOC (Management of Change) tickets.
4. Quarterly: archive the safety evidence package with RFC3161 signing.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **No heartbeats from SIS controllers.** The Edge Hub cannot reach the SIS network. SIS networks are typically air-gapped or on dedicated VLANs with strict firewall rules. Fix: verify network connectivity from the Edge Hub to the SIS controller on Modbus TCP port 502. If air-gapped, use a data diode (unidirectional gateway) to allow SIS data out without allowing any traffic back in.

• **`interlock_state` always shows "unknown".** The `props.conf` field alias doesn't match the vendor's field name. Each SIS vendor uses different terminology: Triconex uses "safety_state", HIMA uses "operating_mode", Honeywell uses "system_status". Fix: check the raw events: `index=ot_safety sourcetype=sis:status | head 5 | fields *safety* *state* *mode* *status*`. Map the correct field name in `props.conf`.

• **Excessive false positives from SIL 1 interlocks.** SIL 1 interlocks are frequently bypassed during normal operations (low safety criticality). Fix: filter the alert to SIL ≥ 2: `| where sil_rating >= 2 OR (sil_rating=1 AND bypass_count > 3)`. This focuses on high-SIL interlocks while still catching excessive bypasses on SIL 1.

• **Change ticket join fails — ServiceNow `number` format mismatch.** The SIS change log records `change_ticket=MOC-2026-042` but ServiceNow uses `number=CHG0012345`. Fix: create a mapping lookup `moc_to_snow.csv` with `moc_number, snow_number` and join through it. Or standardise: require engineers to enter the ServiceNow `CHG` number in the SIS engineering workstation's change ticket field.

• **Modbus polling interferes with SIS controller.** Excessive Modbus polling (e.g., every 1 second) can impact SIS controller CPU and communication channels. This is a safety concern — the monitoring system must not degrade the safety system's performance. Fix: follow the SIS vendor's guidance on maximum polling frequency. Typically, 30-60 second intervals are acceptable. Never use Modbus write function codes (05, 06, 15, 16) on SIS controllers from the monitoring system.

• **Proof test results not appearing.** IEC 61511 requires periodic proof tests of SIS functions. The proof test results may be recorded in a separate system (SAP PM, Maximo) rather than in the SIS controller logs. Fix: ingest proof test records from the maintenance management system via HEC or CSV lookup. Add a complementary search that checks proof test cadence against the SIL register's required test interval.
grandmaExplanation: We watch the automatic shutoff gear that keeps plants from hurting people. When someone sidesteps that protection without a proper signed maintenance ticket, or when tests fall overdue, we raise a clear flag so experts can fix it before something breaks for real.
